title: Progressive breathlessness and chest pressure with hypotension

chart.cc: Shortness of breath and chest pressure
chart.oneLiner: 58F presents with several days of progressive dyspnea, vague chest pressure, and lightheadedness, now hypotensive and tachycardic.

opening: I can't catch my breath and my chest feels tight and heavy. I keep feeling like I'm going to pass out.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old woman, anxious and uncomfortable, sitting upright and breathing fast. Speaks in short sentences. Cooperative but tires easily.

HPI:
- Several days of progressive shortness of breath, now present at rest and worse when lying flat.
- Dull central chest pressure/heaviness, not sharply positional, no clear exertional trigger.
- Lightheadedness and a near-fainting feeling, especially on standing.
- Reduced exercise tolerance and fatigue over the past week or two.
- Had a 'flu-like' viral illness about two weeks ago (only if asked about recent illness).
- Treated for breast cancer 3 years ago and recently told it may have come back; has been having scans (only if asked about cancer history / recent medical workup).
- NO tearing/ripping pain, NO pain radiating to the back, NO calf pain or swelling, NO recent long travel or surgery, NO fevers/chills now, NO cough productive of blood, NO crushing exertional pain or diaphoresis.

OLDCARTS: onset gradual over several days; central chest; constant pressure with associated breathlessness; dull/heavy quality; worse lying flat and with exertion, no clear relief; severity 5/10 pressure but very breathless and faint.

PMH: breast cancer (treated 3 years ago, possible recurrence under workup), hypertension. MEDICATIONS: amlodipine; no anticoagulants. ALLERGIES: none. FAMILY: noncontributory. SOCIAL: never-smoker, rare alcohol, retired teacher.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: anxious, mildly diaphoretic, tachypneic, sitting forward.
- Vitals: BP 86/68, HR 124, RR 24, 37.3°C, SpO2 95% RA. A PULSUS PARADOXUS of about 18 mmHg is present (systolic drops markedly on inspiration) — revealed on careful blood-pressure measurement / checking pulsus.
- Neck: JVP MARKEDLY ELEVATED with a prominent x descent and blunted y descent (revealed if JVP examined).
- Cardiac: HEART SOUNDS are DISTANT/MUFFLED; tachycardic, regular; no friction rub today; PMI difficult to palpate.
- Lungs: CLEAR bilaterally to auscultation (important pertinent negative — no rales/edema despite dyspnea).
- Extremities: cool peripheries with sluggish capillary refill; NO peripheral edema, NO calf tenderness or asymmetric swelling.
- Abdomen: soft, nontender.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent viral illness or the cancer history/possible recurrence unless asked about recent illness, cancer, or recent medical workup.
- The elevated JVP is revealed only if the neck/JVP is examined; the muffled heart sounds only if the heart is auscultated; the pulsus paradoxus only if blood pressure/pulsus is carefully checked.
- Lungs are CLEAR — say so if the chest is auscultated (this is a key negative).
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Troponin: Mildly elevated at 0.06 ng/mL, flat on serial draws (no rising/dynamic pattern to suggest evolving MI).
labs.BNP: Mildly elevated, nonspecific; does not explain the degree of shock.
labs.BMP: Cr mildly elevated consistent with poor forward flow/prerenal state; otherwise unremarkable.
labs.Ddimer: Elevated but nonspecific; CT pulmonary angiogram (if obtained) shows NO pulmonary embolism and incidentally notes a large pericardial effusion.
labs.echo: Large circumferential pericardial effusion with TAMPONADE PHYSIOLOGY: diastolic collapse of the right atrium and right ventricle, exaggerated respiratory variation in transvalvular inflow, and a dilated, non-collapsing (plethoric) IVC. Confirms the diagnosis.
labs.CBC: WBC and Hgb within normal range; no leukocytosis to suggest purulent pericarditis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and beat-to-beat electrical alternans of the QRS amplitude.
ekg.verify: Source a tracing matching: sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans. Self-host with credit to litfl.com; do not reuse the prior tracing.

ekg.clinicalVignette: A 60-year-old woman presents with progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and chest fullness after a recent viral pericarditis.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lung fields (no pulmonary edema).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: enlarged globular water-bottle cardiac silhouette with clear lungs. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing enlarged globular water-bottle cardiac silhouette with clear lungs
searchTerms: water bottle cardiac silhouette pericardial effusion
globular heart chest x-ray pericardial effusion
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 64-year-old man presents with worsening breathlessness, distended neck veins, and muffled heart sounds.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this dyspnea/chest pressure with hypotension (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Cardiac tamponade / large pericardial effusion (leading, 9). Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock (CANT-MISS, 5). Massive pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS, 3). Tension pneumothorax (core, 2). Acute decompensated heart failure (core, 2). Sepsis/other distributive shock (bonus, 1). Names obstructive shock with elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, hypotension, and pulsus paradoxus pointing to tamponade (core, 3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Cardiac tamponade (large pericardial effusion causing obstructive shock). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock; Massive pulmonary embolism. Also reasonable: Tension pneumothorax; Acute decompensated heart failure.

criticalActions.item: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock

coreActions.item: Names obstructive shock with elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, hypotension, and pulsus paradoxus pointing to tamponade

mustNotMiss: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Bedside/urgent echocardiogram to assess for effusion and tamponade physiology (CANT-MISS, 5). EKG (core, 3). CXR (core, 2). Troponin and serial cardiac biomarkers (core, 2). BMP/renal function and CBC (core, 2). D-dimer or CTPA if PE still considered (bonus, 1). PENALTY: giving a diuretic or nitrate for presumed heart failure before excluding tamponade (worsens preload-dependent shock) (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Troponin; BNP; BMP; D-dimer; Echocardiogram; CBC.

bonusActions.item: D-dimer or CTPA if PE still considered

penalties.item: giving a diuretic or nitrate before excluding tamponade (worsens preload-dependent shock)

optionalTests: D-dimer or CTPA if PE still considered
contraindicatedTests: giving a diuretic or nitrate before excluding tamponade (worsens preload-dependent shock)
orderSensitiveActions: giving a diuretic or nitrate before excluding tamponade (worsens preload-dependent shock)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans. CREDIT: rate & rhythm — sinus tachycardia (3); identifies LOW QRS VOLTAGE (4); recognizes ELECTRICAL ALTERNANS — beat-to-beat QRS amplitude variation (4); links the triad to a large effusion/tamponade (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: misreading as STEMI and activating the cath lab, or calling it normal (-3).
keyFindings: links findings to large pericardial effusion/tamponade
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans — classic for a large pericardial effusion with tamponade.
commonMistakes: missing the electrical alternans and reporting only sinus tachycardia
misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and beat-to-beat electrical alternans — the classic triad of a large pericardial effusion with tamponade.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans suggesting a large effusion/tamponade.

coreActions: links findings to large pericardial effusion/tamponade

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misreads as STEMI

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = enlarged globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lungs. CREDIT: identifies the enlarged/globular cardiac silhouette (6); notes clear lungs without pulmonary edema (a key negative arguing against LV failure) and that a large effusion is suggested (4). PENALTY: calling it normal or mistaking it for cardiomegaly from heart failure with edema (-3).
keyFindings: clear lung fields without pulmonary edema (argues against LV failure)
criticalFindings: Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lungs, suggesting a large pericardial effusion.
commonMistakes: mistaking the silhouette for cardiomegaly of heart failure and expecting pulmonary edema
steps.idealAnswer: Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lung fields — suggestive of a large pericardial effusion; the clear lungs argue against LV failure.

criticalActions: Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lungs, suggesting a large pericardial effusion.
coreActions: clear lung fields without pulmonary edema (argues against LV failure)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Hypotension with pulsus paradoxus, elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, clear lungs, EKG with low voltage and electrical alternans, a water-bottle heart on CXR, and an echo showing a large effusion with right-heart diastolic collapse and a plethoric IVC. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Cardiac tamponade from a large pericardial effusion (obstructive shock) (6). Cites Beck's triad plus pulsus paradoxus and the echo tamponade physiology; explicitly excludes ACS (flat troponin, no dynamic ischemic EKG) and PE (no PE on imaging), and notes clear lungs argue against LV failure (4).
mustNotMiss: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH pericardial disease/tamponade: urgent pericardiocentesis (echo-guided) is the definitive treatment (CANT-MISS, 8); IV volume expansion to support a preload-dependent circulation as a temporizing measure (CANT-MISS, 5); urgent cardiology/cardiac surgery consult (4); continuous monitoring in a high-acuity setting with avoidance of intubation/positive-pressure ventilation if possible (3); send pericardial fluid for analysis and treat the underlying cause, e.g. malignant effusion (3); pericardial drain/window for recurrent or malignant effusion (2). PENALTY: diuretics or vasodilators/nitrates that drop preload (-3); delaying drainage in an unstable patient to obtain further imaging (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: This is decompensating tamponade: resuscitate and drain. Give IV fluids to support preload while arranging URGENT echo-guided pericardiocentesis (definitive). Call cardiology/cardiac surgery immediately. Manage in a high-acuity/monitored setting; avoid diuretics, nitrates, and (if possible) positive-pressure ventilation, all of which reduce preload. Send pericardial fluid for cytology/culture and treat the underlying cause (likely malignant effusion given breast-cancer history); consider a pericardial drain or surgical window for recurrent/malignant effusions.

criticalActions.item: IV volume expansion to support preload (temporizing)

coreActions.item: High-acuity monitoring; avoid positive-pressure ventilation if possible
Pericardial fluid analysis and treat underlying cause (malignant effusion)

bonusActions.item: Pericardial drain/window for recurrent or malignant effusion

penalties.item: Diuretics or vasodilators/nitrates that drop preload
Delaying drainage in an unstable patient for further imaging

requiredActions: IV volume expansion to support preload (temporizing)
optionalActions: High-acuity monitoring; avoid positive-pressure ventilation if possible
Pericardial fluid analysis and treat underlying cause (malignant effusion)
Pericardial drain/window for recurrent or malignant effusion
unsafeActions: Diuretics or vasodilators/nitrates that drop preload
Delaying drainage in an unstable patient for further imaging

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58F presents with several days of progressive dyspnea, vague chest pressure, and lightheadedness, now hypotensive and tachycardic. The diagnosis is Cardiac tamponade, reached by eliciting the focused history (including a malignancy/recurrence and recent viral illness), performing the targeted exam (elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus, clear lungs), and interpreting the directed workup (EKG low voltage with electrical alternans, water-bottle CXR, confirmatory echo) while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neck: JVP MARKEDLY ELEVATED with a prominent x descent and blunted y descent.
Cardiac: HEART SOUNDS are DISTANT/MUFFLED; tachycardic, regular; no friction rub today; PMI difficult to palpate.
Vitals: a PULSUS PARADOXUS of about 18 mmHg is present (systolic drops markedly on inspiration).
Lungs: CLEAR bilaterally to auscultation (no rales/edema despite dyspnea).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (cancer/recurrence, recent viral illness) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings (elevated JVP, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus, clear lungs).
Build a differential led by Cardiac tamponade while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes (ACS, massive PE).
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and the confirmatory echo.
Commit to Cardiac tamponade and manage with stabilization (IV fluids), definitive drainage (pericardiocentesis), consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Diuretics or vasodilators/nitrates that drop preload
Delaying drainage in an unstable patient for further imaging
managementPearls: IV volume expansion to support preload (temporizing)
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans — classic for a large pericardial effusion with tamponade.
Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lungs, suggesting a large pericardial effusion.
criticalManagementItems: IV volume expansion to support preload (temporizing)
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans — classic for a large pericardial effusion with tamponade.
Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lungs, suggesting a large pericardial effusion.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans — classic for a large pericardial effusion with tamponade.
Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette with clear lungs, suggesting a large pericardial effusion.
imageInterpretationPearls: Low voltage plus electrical alternans on the EKG is a high-yield clue to a large effusion — name it explicitly.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives — clear lungs with shock points away from LV failure.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
